Alicia Levine: Okay. So the raw materials for what makes U.S. exceptionalism are here. We have businesses forming every day. We have people with great ideas getting funded by the VC community. We didn't talk about the venture community in the U.S. The venture community funding a great idea is so developed in the U.S. and just doesn't happen elsewhere.

Great innovators come to the U.S. to build companies because they can get funded here. We are a nation of investors. 60% of households are exposed to equity markets. This is what we do. People talk about the stocks. I mean, the college kids talk about the stocks. This is amazing.

We fund companies. We provide capital as a society. You can't replace that. You just can't replace that kind of culture of believing in innovation. So that's the great part. You also have some incentives in the One Big Beautiful Bill for innovation in CapEx. We like that. We like AI. We like that. And we like the diffusion of it in all sectors, making every sector more profitable and more productive.

So we like that. So what's the problem? What could go wrong? A couple of things. I think that the debt and the deficits are an issue because it'll keep long-term interest rates higher. And so we know that, for instance, all borrowing is based against the 10-year bond, and the 10-year yield is hanging out there in a place where mortgages are 6.8%, 7%, very hard for first time homebuyers. We know this.

And so all the spending that Washington has done, let's call it for 10 years, is showing up in some of the 10-year yields off of which every borrowing happens. So we're concerned about that. Is it too expensive to borrow?

We're a little bit concerned also about the labor market. There are some cracks in the labor market. It's getting harder for new college graduates to find a job. I think there's some sorting skill sets. That's something to keep our eye on. We do think the labor market is in equilibrium, meaning the supply is coming down and so the hiring of under 100,000 per month could be okay if the supply is lower. That's an equilibrium, supply and demand.

But you want to keep an eye on it for growth because if it goes too low, you then have to question where's growth going if our labor force is ever so slightly ticking down? So we're looking at the labor market. We're looking at retail sales. A consumption is 70% of GDP. And we're really focused on what's happening with bond yields because that's where borrowing goes.

So great foundation, great fundamentals, few spots out there we want to keep our eye on. But overall, we're invested, we're fully allocated. And I think we've been on the right side of calling this really since April. We know markets respond more quickly than policy itself. And the mistake that investors made was waiting for clarity.

We never have clarity ever. There's only something. What do I know? The market trades off of earnings and a multiple. And if I have earnings and I have nominal growth, I'm going to have a higher market.

Mike Larson: Well, Alicia, really thank you so much for your insights. I appreciate you taking some time out here.

Alicia Levine: Thanks, Mike.

Originally Published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: New Pacific Metals: Large Silver Mines In Bolivia, But Huge Capital Needed, Shares Could Retreat (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
A Hold Recommendation For New Pacific Metals Corp.

This article contains a Hold recommendation on the NYSE-listed shares of New Pacific Metals Corp. (NYSE:NEWP), a Canadian company with plans to mine payable ounces of silver from large open-pit mines in Bolivia. The hold recommendation on this stock represents a slight downgrade from the buy recommendation in our previous article.

We believe it is appropriate to give our “hold” recommendation now that NYSE shares of New Pacific Metals, or NEWP, have delivered strong results, driven by a sharp increase in silver prices, to the extent that they are now overbought.

At this point, the downward momentum could be building and cause a sharp but temporary decline in the stock price. The company's progress on its mining project is reassuring and pleasing to the markets, and its liquidity reserves are sufficient to get things through a few more years.

But to move further up, the stock needs room to realise its full potential, and so we are already seeing that, healthily, it pulls back from this point.

The Stock Price Performance of New Pacific Metals Corp. Shares Since Our Last Article

Since our last article on November 24, 2024, NYSE-listed shares of New Pacific Metals Corp. have posted a positive change of +16.13%, outperforming the S&P 500, which was up +6.12% at the time of writing.

This stock is exposed to the volatility of silver prices, but unlike the metal itself, it does not exhibit a long-term upward trend. As we will explain later in this analysis, this situation will continue until the company receives external capital, at which point we expect a turning point. Until then, we recommend increasing positions or starting positions only during dramatic price declines. This tactic helps mitigate the downside risk associated with volatility and minimises the risks associated with the stock's low liquidity. Let us assume that a position contains too many shares acquired at an unfavourable price. Let us also assume that unforeseen circumstances force the investor to lower the selling price to well below the average buying price because the market can barely meet the demand. In this case, it would be an understatement to call the investor's performance poor. His negligence in not paying adequate attention to the following statistics could very likely force him to suffer a relatively large loss due to poorly managed volatility. The statistics consist of this: According to the “Trading Data” section of the Seeking Alpha website, New Pacific Metals has an Average Volume (3 months) of 272,943 shares, which is very low because a search online will show that the general trend in the US stock market is that many more shares are traded, well over one million per day on average per company in the US stock market.

On top of this, if investors had profited from lower stock prices in the market, as we suggest, they would likely have ended up with a greater gain than 16.13%.

New Pacific Metals Corp. And Its 20 MLN Oz Annual Silver Production Target in Bolivia

In its March 2025 quarter and nine months ended March 31, 2025, report, New Pacific Metals Corp. described itself as follows:

New Pacific is a Canadian exploration and development company with three precious metal projects in Bolivia. The Company's flagship Silver Sand project has the potential to be developed into one of the world's largest silver mines. The Company is also advancing its robust, high-margin silver-lead-zinc Carangas project. Additionally, a discovery drill program was completed at Silverstrike in 2022.

Bolivia is a risky country for mining and mineral exploration companies (see Sprott's map) due to territorial disputes between mining cooperatives. The northwest of the country has been most recently affected by this problem, while New Pacific Metals Corp.'s flagship property, the 100%-owned Silver Sand property, is located in southwest Bolivia. This also must be said.

Sprott is a global asset manager with decades of experience in physical investments in metals and minerals, as well as close relationships and expertise in the mining industry.

New Pacific Metals Corp. By Project

The Silver Sand Project is located 33 km northeast of Potosi, Bolivia, covers an area of 5.42 km², and is 100% owned by New Pacific Metals Corp. The project is in the pre-feasibility stage and targets an open-pit silver mine producing over 15 million ounces of silver (Moz) annually during the first three years of production, with an average production Life-of-Mine (LOM) of over 12 million ounces per annum.

The property hosts estimated proven and probable mineral reserves of 52 million tonnes (Mt), grading 105 grams of silver per tonne of ore (g/t), for a total metal content of 175.4 million ounces of silver. The Silver Sand deposit will be developed using conventional open-pit mining. The entire process, from mine to silver doré bar, includes crushing, grinding, and milling of the Run-Of-Mine (ROM) ore stockpiles, as well as cyanide leaching, subsequent decantation, zinc precipitation, copper separation, and finally smelting to produce the final silver doré. The complex will have a tailings storage facility (TSF) to store the filtered and sealed tailings from silver production. The property is connected to the city of Potosí by paved roads. Other important utilities for the complex include electricity from the public grid and water from nearby reservoirs. Reservoir replacement options are also available in the event of water shortages.

A preliminary economic assessment (PEA) was completed in January 2023, and the pre-feasibility study (PFS) results were announced in January 2023 for the Silver Sand Project. This total investment of $358 million is necessary because the Silver Sand Project has a 13-year production life. As of December 31, 2024, Silver Sand's expenditures totalled $89,609,609, or 25% of total expenditures, and required further financing through the issuance of equity or debt from lenders.

The Carangas Project is located 193 km southwest of Oruro, Bolivia, and is indirectly (100%) owned by New Pacific Metals Corp. The Carangas Project is a silver and base metals project. Under a 30-year mining agreement with the private Bolivian company Mineral Granville, New Pacific Metals has agreed to bear all costs and receive 98% of the operating profit once Carangas commences operations.

A PEA dated October 2024 is available for the Carangas Project. This project forecasts a 16-year LOM for total production of 106 million ounces of payable silver (or 6.63 million ounces of silver annually), 620 million pounds of payable zinc, and 382 million pounds of payable lead. Caranga's polymetallic mineral resources total 214.9 million tonnes of indicated mineral resources, including 205.3 million ounces of silver, 1,588.2 thousand ounces of gold, 1,444.9 million pounds of lead, 2,653.7 million pounds of zinc, and 112.6 million pounds of copper. This totals 559.8 million ounces of silver equivalent (AgEq). These estimates were announced in September 2023.

The entire operation includes ore mining, ROM ore storage, subsequent crushing, stockpile processing (in the last two years of operation), a SAG ball mill (SABC) circuit, and a sequential sulfide flotation plant for the separation of silver/lead and zinc. The complex includes a storage facility for the filtered and sealed tailings from silver production. A total investment of US$324 million is necessary because the Carangas Project has a 16-year production life. As of December 30, 2024, the Carangas Project's expenditures totalled US$20,843,608, representing approximately 6.5% of total expenditures. Further financing through the issuance of equity or debt securities was required.

In addition, New Pacific Metals Corp. owns a 44.5 km² area at an elevation of 4,000 to 4,500 m at Silverstrike, approximately 140 km southwest of La Paz, Bolivia, or approximately 450 km northwest of New Pacific's Silver Sand project.

Under a 30-year agreement with a private Bolivian company dated December 2019, New Pacific Metals Corp. is obligated to bear 100% of the Silverstrike project's future expenditures, including exploration, development, and subsequent mining production costs. New Pacific completed a 6,000-meter discovery drilling program at the Silverstrike project in 2022, with the first holes intersecting broad, oxidised gold mineralisation near surface, leading to a mineral potential similar to Carangas. As of December 31, 2024, Silverstrike's expenditures totalled $4.9 million.

Amazing Upside Potential Through The Project Portfolio

In its Management Discussion and Analysis for the three and nine months ended March 31, 2025 (figures in US dollars), New Pacific Metals Corp. highlights the following economic aspects of the Silver Sands Project and says:

 Post-tax NPV at a 5% discount rate of $740 million and IRR of 37% at a base case price of $24.00/oz silver

 13 years mine life, excluding the 2 years pre-production period, producing approximately 157 Moz of silver. Annual silver production exceeds 15 Moz in years one through three, with LOM average annual silver production exceeding 12 Moz

 Initial capital costs of $358 million and a post-tax payback of 1.9 years (from the start of production) at $24.00/oz silver

 Average LOM AISC of $10.69/oz silver

Regarding the Carangas project, the company highlights the following economic aspects and explains:

 Post-tax NPV (5%) of $501 million and IRR of 26% at a base case price of $24.00/ oz silver, $1.25/ lb zinc, and $0.95/lb lead

 16-year LOM, excluding 2 years of pre-production, producing approximately 106 Moz of payable silver, 620 Mlbs of payable zinc, and 382 Mlbs of payable lead

 Initial capital costs of $324 million and a post-tax payback of 3.2 years

 Average LOM AISC of $7.60/oz silver, net of by-products

 Approximately 500 direct permanent jobs will be created from the Carangas Project

In our view, these projects may be of great interest to investors/financiers because of their expected future profitability. As evidenced by an Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of 37% for the Silver Sands project and 26% for the Carangas project, their profitability is very competitive compared to many of the projects we have reviewed to date. As these other projects generally had IRRs in the 15% to 35% range, the Silver Sands project and Carangas stand in the mid to upper end of economically sound precious metals mining projects.

Also, it is worth noting that their IRRs were calculated based on an assumed silver price of $24 per ounce. The price of silver has grown quite a lot from there. The current silver price per ounce is more than 1.5 times higher, and based on projections, the price is expected to be significantly higher than current levels due to favourable silver demand factors, making increasingly visible the inability of the supply to keep up with the demand.

The two major future silver mining projects in Bolivia could thus be even more profitable than commonly assumed. This information typically contributes to market sentiment. Since market sentiment is a guide for NYSE stocks, given the potential for higher earnings scenarios just discussed for Silver Sands and Carangas as silver prices rise, a market sentiment that contributes to the stock's rise, facilitating its recovery when to restart from a volatility-induced downswing, in our opinion, will also work for New Pacific Metals.

The Silver Price And Its Outlook

At the time of writing, Silver Spot Price (XAGUSD:CUR) was $38.94/oz, up 35.14% year-to-date (YTD) and 35.34% year-over-year. This price was driven by investors' enthusiasm for the metal safe’s haven properties to protect the positive development of their asset portfolios from the uncertainty of conflicts and global trade tensions.

Furthermore, a new force is giving the silver price additional momentum: the safe-haven silver is now significantly cheaper than its shinier cousin, gold. Much of the investment demand that previously flowed into the safe haven of gold is now being diverted into silver. Combined with robust industrial demand for the metal in the areas of electrification, electric vehicles, AI data centres, and solar energy, this investment demand is reinforcing the upward momentum. And that's not all: The Silver Institute is reporting a shortage in the silver market as global supply will once again be insufficient to meet strong industrial demand for the metal. This imbalance between supply and demand creates strong upward pressure on the silver price. This alone could be enough to persuade investors who may have still been sceptical to overcome their last reservations and decisively turn to silver, further boosting demand for the grey metal. These are more or less the reasons why the price of silver will continue reaching new highs from its current level.

As per silver price forecasts, major US banks predict a price of $40 per ounce by 2026, and as the chart published on goldsilver-dotcom shows, the price of silver will reach $75 per ounce in 2027. These analysts particularly emphasise the positive factor of strong industrial demand for electric vehicles.

The Next Tasks of New Pacific Metals Corp. With the Silver Sands Project, as well as Carangas and Silverstrike

Silver Sand: Provided that the Silver Sand Project open-pit mine, recognised by the Bolivian Ministry of Environment and Water, successfully completes its socialisation process with the local communities, the company will undertake the following tasks.

In its Management Discussion and Analysis for the three and nine months ended March 31, 2025, New Pacific Metals Corp. highlights the tasks and says:

 Obtain surface rights through long-term land lease agreements;

 Finalize a resettlement and compensation plan for impacted families; and

 Implement measures to safeguard cultural and historical heritage.

Carangas: New Pacific is working to obtain the necessary permits for the project. The feasibility study for the Carangas project is expected to be completed by April 2026, compared to the initial 12 to 18 months from the PEA in October 2024. Additionally, a mine development agreement will be signed with the local community to convert the exploration permit into a mining permit. According to the company and other online sources, this status will not be achieved until the second half of 2025 at the earliest.

Silverstrike: The company is likely to conduct additional drilling campaigns in the future.

The Financial Condition For Supporting The Tasks

In its management discussion and analysis for the three and nine months ended March 31, 2025, New Pacific Metals Corp. states:

Management believes that the Company has sufficient funds to support its normal exploration and operating requirements for at least, but not limited to, the next twelve months.

We expect longer autonomy with existing financial resources: New Pacific Metals Corp. had cash and cash equivalents totalling $17.4 million in the March 2025 quarter. The company also held bonds and equity securities, but these are small compared to the available liquidity. This liquidity was raised through the previous offering of its own common shares to the public. The company has not yet raised any debt capital.

New Pacific Metals Corp. currently produces no silver or other base metals and therefore generates no revenue. However, operating expenses are incurred to maintain its projects; these amounted to US$1.36 million for the three months ended March 31, 2025, and US$4.56 million for the nine months ended March 31, 2025. Therefore, it is not a surprise that the company incurred a loss.

In its financial report for the three and nine months ended March 31, 2025 (all figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated), the company states:

Net loss attributable to equity holders of the Company for the three and nine months ended March 31, 2025 was $0.86 million and $2.86 million or $0.01 per share and $0.02 per share, respectively (three and nine months ended March 31, 2024 – net loss of $1.27 million and $4.54 million or $0.01 and $0.03 per share, respectively).

Now let's see the movements in the cash flows during the 9 months through the March 2025 quarter: Approximately $2.6 million was used in operations in the nine months to March 2025, primarily to cover the operating expenses.

Cash flows used in investing activities amounted to approximately $2.3 million for capital expenditures in the nine months to March 2025, for asset improvements. These refer primarily to New Pacific's flagship Silver Sand project, the high-margin Carangas silver-lead zinc project, and the exploration drilling program at Silverstrike. Additional expenditures on these three projects were capitalised, bringing the total net value of property, plant, and equipment to $116.5 million as of March 2025.

The company has not raised any additional capital through the issuance of common stock or through borrowing over the nine months ending March 2025.

Free cash flow was negative (= $2.6 million outflows for operations plus $2.3 million outflows for capital expenditures) and amounted to $4.9 million for the 9 months ending March 2025. This coincided with the change in cash and cash equivalents from $22 million in the June 2024 quarter to $17.1 million in the March 2025 quarter, excluding the results of short-term investments, which reflected an outflow of $0.3 million comparing the June 2024 quarter to the March 2025 quarter.

But short-term investments can also generate inflows because, while fixed-income securities such as bonds and certificates of deposit pay interest, investments in publicly traded stocks are subject to volatile conditions, and gains/losses in the foreign exchange markets are also volatile. Over the past 9 months through the March 2025 quarter, inflows from fixed income assets were more than offset by outflows from investments in publicly traded equities and unfavourable development of foreign exchanges.

With operating and capital expenditures of approximately $6.5 million to $7 million annually, plus a safety margin of an additional $0.5 million per year for possible negative returns on investments in short-term and marketable securities, Pacific Metals requires between $7 million and $7.5 million annually to cover costs and finance its operations. The $17.1 million in cash and cash equivalents as of March 30, 2024, provides the company with financial flexibility for an additional 2.5 years from the March 2025 quarter, i.e., until the December 2026 quarter. The company will then have raised capital through the issuance of common stock and/or borrowings from lenders.

Recent history suggests that the previous financing option does not promise positive long-term appreciation in the value of New Pacific's NYSE shares. On September 25, 2023, a “bought deal financing” of C$35 million was announced at a price of C$2.65 or US$1.96. Compared to that, New Pacific’s current share price on the NYSE doesn’t represent a progression. We suspect that the second option, that is, the participation of debt to finance the needs of New Pacific Metals, could generate strong positive sentiment and lead to long-term appreciation. This is especially true since lenders will only provide capital if the company is very solid and can afford an annual interest expense computed at, for example, more than a 4.4% rate. The 4.4% rate is the current yield on 10-year government bonds as a benchmark for long-term interest rates. If lenders were to lend to New Pacific Metals, it would be clear evidence of the value of the projects in Bolivia, which would be seen as “well worth the money”. Inflationary pressures due to a solid labour market and robust US consumption are leading fixed-income traders to raise the likelihood that the Federal Reserve will keep interest rates within a cautious area. CME FedWatch predicts a 25 basis point rate cut at the FOMC meeting on September 17, 2025, but has lowered the probability from 70% a month ago to a current 60.4%. Because market expectations about the Fed’s future interest rate policy affect long-term interest rates, borrowing costs are bound to stay elevated for longer than initially expected. As was the case with Skeena Resources Limited, which secured a $750 million Orion financing package for its Eskay Creek gold mining project in northwestern British Columbia under very tight credit conditions, in our view, the equity market will probably also be very happy if New Pacific secures a loan from lenders.

The Stock Price Is Overbought: A Decline In The Stock Price Could Be In The Offing

At the time of writing, the shares of New Pacific Metals Corp., trading under the symbol “NEWP”, were trading on the NYSE at $1.73 per share, representing a market capitalisation of $308.48 million.

The shares are trading above the MA Ribbon and close to the upper end of the 52-week range of $0.93 to $2.05 per share.

The RSI index indicates that the stock is overbought, and investors who want to own or add shares in this company should wait for a sharp decline in market value.

We've identified three factors that could drive the stock price down in the short term, as they do not bode well for the price of silver. This stock price and the silver price have a positive correlation:

  • Inflation is in its final stages, but it is not easy to complete its return to the 2% target, as a strong labour market and improved consumer confidence in the US augment the risk of inflation. Against this backdrop, US central bankers appear to be keeping interest rates elevated. Higher interest rates dampen the demand for zero-yielding silver bullion, to the advantage of US Treasury bonds, as they instead generate interest income. Although this could be just temporary, a less optimistic opinion about silver is not beneficial for New Pacific Metals, which plans to build large silver mines in Bolivia.
  • Analysts like Julian Emanuel, head of strategy at Evercore ISI, fear that a minor disappointment following the strong rebound from April's lows could lead to remarkable declines in the US stock market. New Pacific's stock, with a 24M beta of 2.50 (see Seeking Alpha's “Risk” section), will pay a steep price due to its tendency to amplify stock market headwinds.
  • Probably the above view is shared by other analysts who see signs that the US stock market's rally is losing momentum, even though it has the appearance of being happy to bask up there at the heights it reached after its march towards record levels in July.

Conclusion

New Pacific Metals Corp. is a Vancouver, Canada-based company that plans to develop large silver deposits in Bolivia to produce and sell the metal. The metal is experiencing tremendous growth due to several robust demand factors.

NEWP shares are strongly linked to the price of silver, which has a promising future as a key component of electrification, electric vehicles, the energy transition, and AI data centres.

The price of silver will also benefit because investors flock to safe haven properties of the metal in the face of risks and uncertainties, and these properties are offered at visibly lower prices than gold. The stock will continue to be subject to the volatility of the silver price until an inflection point is reached. At that point, shares will tend to rise despite ups and downs. We believe the inflection point will occur when New Pacific Corp. receives debt financing from lenders. Given the difficult credit conditions and the looming threat of insolvency, the approval of the loan applications is a clear sign that lenders are pleased with the Bolivian projects and are convinced of their worth. Thus, New Pacific has a fortune in its hands, and it has the money, so it can work to develop that value.

New Pacific Corp. requires approximately $685 million in capital to start silver production in Bolivia. This has so far covered approximately 15% of the total investment costs.

The shares are overbought, and the bearish momentum could intensify.

data.title: Centene: Pricing In Complete Obliteration
data.description:
In our investing careers, we have not seen a sector, or a sub-sector if you will, so abandoned as we have seen in managed care insurance within the healthcare sector. Everywhere you look, there are negative headlines, earnings revisions lower, down guides, or pulled guides altogether. And all of these names continue to fall, almost all in sympathy, headline after headline. One name that we cannot remember being this cheap in the last decade is Centene Corporation (NYSE:CNC). Centene is a name we have traded a few times for solid returns in the past at BAD BEAT Investing. While we have entered into a competitor for what we think is a 12-24 month turnaround, CNC has the potential to be perhaps a generational buying opportunity in our opinion.

Record pessimism

At record pessimism, we believe buyers will be rewarded in the future. It may take a change of power in Congress, which is potentially just over a year away, but we do not see the models changing long-term. Every so often the sector takes a beat down on regulation or reimbursement issues, but this beat down is larger than we have seen before. We will not go into detail on what is happening with competitors, but there is headline after headline. Centene has pretty much 'kitchen-sinked' it at this point, and now, it is moving with peers as the selloff continues. We think that money can be made long-term in the sector. Consider buying Centene, but recognize it is tough to pinpoint a bottom. Purchases here and each $2 block lower is an investment approach to consider. Let us discuss.

Headwinds galore

First, headwinds are regulatory in nature. The Big Beautiful Bill did not do insurers any favors. There is exposure here to slowing end markets in Medicaid and the Affordable Care Act exchanges. There is also a major headwind of what looks like a possible expiry of exchange subsidies this year. The belief is that there will be building enrollment headwinds for Medicaid in the years ahead. That remains to be seen however. Changes can always come from future Congresses. The 'work requirements' are cited as a headwind.

The biggest reason Centene stock has cratered, aside from falling alongside peers that have their own issues, is that they had pulled their guidance, given a huge step-up in medical costs. Similar to what we have covered previously in competitors. We were not at all surprised. However, we were surprised that the market had not priced this in, not even close. One could argue we are now overshooting to the downside, and a reversion to the mean trade could be setting up. Those are special situations we like to take advantage of at BAD BEAT Investing.

Why is it trading so poorly?

As you can see, a decade of gains has been wiped out. Why is it so bad? Upon reviewing the costs here, the company analyzed the 2025 industry Health Insurance Marketplace data from an independent actuarial firm, covering 22 of Centene's 29 Marketplace states, and representing approximately 72% of the Company's Marketplace membership. Centene's interpretation of the data was very bearish, and after having discussions with the actuarial firm, Centene determined that the overall market growth in these 22 states is lower than expected. As we saw with other competitors, patients are also much more ill than expected. Given the higher morbidity, the costs here are "materially inconsistent with, the company's assumptions." As such, it sees much higher costs ahead with an adjusted diluted EPS impact of approximately $2.75 for the year. That is huge folks a little more than 1/3 of earnings that were expected. And while there was no data for the other 7 states, the company assumes similar trends, and perhaps a further reduction in EPS. What this means is the EPS hit could be even more, something that Q2 results seem to indicate. What can it do?

What can the company do?

So what can the company do? Well, looking ahead, it has to reprice plans. So, for the 2026 Marketplace plan year, Centene is refilling its 2026 Marketplace rates to reflect a much higher projected baseline of Marketplace benefit usage than previously expected. These corrective pricing actions for 2026 will happen in most states. Much of the higher Medicaid costs are coming from behavioral health, home health, and higher dollar prescriptions, and were very notable in New York and Florida.

There was one sliver of good news in all of this. With this review and awful ratcheting down of expectations, Centene's Medicare Advantage and Medicare Prescription Drug Plan businesses were cited as "performing better than expected in the second quarter 2025". The company is also focusing on its own internal expense reduction, including improving selling, general, and administrative expense leverage on its consolidated premium and service revenue growth.

Earnings update

While a lot of this quarter was known from the pulled guide, we now just got an update in the form of the full earnings here. One would think that everything is priced in now as shares crater further, again. At the time of this writing, the stock is down 15% premarket. It is going to take time. The knife has fallen, and fallen again. No, we do not buy into the comments that "the company will be bankrupt" that we are seeing being made in our colleagues' columns on the matter.

Some key highlights for the just-reported earnings are noteworthy. First, the positive. Revenue was a big beat. It came in at $48.74 billion, a $4.5 billion beat against consensus. It was also up 22.3% from last year. Premium and service revenues increased 18% to $42.5 billion from $36.0 billion last year. But the good news stops there. The earnings themselves, on a per share basis, were a very rare loss. EPS missed by $0.39 and was a loss of $0.16. Nearly unheard of. Why? It all comes down to the health benefits ratio, people using more care, at 93%. We do not have a reason why this year there really is so much more usage, the aforementioned drug costs and behavioral health claims notwithstanding. It appears that the company is already looking forward to 2026.

In terms of the balance sheet, at the end of the quarter, the company's investments and deposits were $37.5 billion and it also had $234 million of cash and cash equivalents. That said, Medical claims liabilities totaled $20.1 billion, with big expenses coming due. The company's days in claims payable were 47 days, a decrease of two days as compared to the first quarter of 2025 driven by the timing and types of claims, as well as the impact of state-directed payments. And as a reminder there is a good chunk of debt here, but it is easily serviced by cash flows. Total debt was $17.6 billion, which included no borrowings on its $4.0 billion revolving credit facility.

Conference call key notes

The company is also providing 2025 earnings expectations. But it is doing so on the call/presentation that we are listening to as we write. The company of course is "disappointed" as they stated on the call. They are focused on getting the marketplace back to profitability. The Medicare Advantage business looks to be breakeven in 2027. The Marketplace book continues to go. But the shortfall in risk-adjusted revenue persists. The high utilization is now incorporated into the full-year outlook which was updated on the call.

The company also updated the aforementioned announcement based on data from the actuary. Marketplace earnings will now be impacted by $2.4 billion, versus $1.8 billion. Three things are driving this. Higher than expected healthier members have left the marketplace, after pricing was implemented. New sign-ups have higher morbidity (still not known why), and more aggressive provider coding on top of more usage has led to massive increases in utilization. The product will now run below breakeven for the year. The pricing will be adjusted in 2026. 2026 pricing has been filed in many states, which will return the company to profitability. The goal is to reprice the entire book. For Medicaid, it also fell short of expectations with a 94.9% benefit ratio (due to home health, behavioral health, and drug costs, as was known before).

Finally, as for the outlook for 2025, it is ugly. The market is pricing the stock for the company's obliteration. But the company is pushing for rate adjustments to offset most of this pain. But 2025 will suffer. The figure targeted is $1.75 for the year in EPS. Down from over $7. My how quickly things changed. There is a laundry list of reasons provided that are pressuring margins, as well as rate increases later this year, and utilization.

For us, we view this as a so-called blood in the streets moment, where you simply hold your nose and buy. Do you go all in? Of course not. You spread your bets across sectors. Even if you are trading, you still spread your trades. Our thesis is simple. First, regulatory damage has been done. Second, the company is working to adjust pricing to reflect what is happening with utilization. Third, and this is a bit of a pin action assumption, but the proliferation of weight loss drugs should reduce morbidities associated with obesity in the longer-term which should save costs. Usage of behavioral health benefits long-term benefit well-being, which in turn, we believe reduces substance abuse and unhealthy eating. So near-term usage is painful, but long-term, it benefits the health of the populations being served.

Looking ahead

As we look ahead to the year, it is difficult to project EPS for the company. Here we are at $24 a share. Oddly in all of this, we expect revenue to still grow some 7-10% this year. But the costs, of course, as noted will weigh on EPS. EPS is likely to be cut in half this year from last year. Perhaps slightly more. However, if cost savings are put into place further, and usage is even remotely less than expected in the updated projections, EPS could be in the high $2 range, putting shares at 12x FWD EPS. At $1.75 in EPS, this is 13.7x FWD. In a worst-case scenario, the company, if it continues to see ongoing pressures, could see as little as $1 in EPS, meaning an expensive 24x. There are many factors that could drive earnings higher; however, the company is looking toward 2026, when a return to growth returns. They fully expect improvement in 2026 in all lines of business versus 2025. Whether the market wants to pay a higher multiple hangs in the balance with the earnings update we will receive. We think investors consider a starter position earnings, and look to add on to further weakness. We think a lot of damage is priced in.

data.title: TYG: Renewables Keep Producing Losses, But Strength In Midstream Offsets It
data.description:
Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation (NYSE:TYG) is a closed-end fund that invests primarily in midstream energy corporations and partnerships. This could be something that investors who are seeking to earn an income off of their assets might find attractive, due to the simple fact that most midstream energy companies boast very high yields. For example, here are the yields of some of the largest midstream energy corporations in North America:

Company Name

Current Yield

Enbridge Inc. (ENB)

6.06%

Kinder Morgan (KMI)

4.36%

Pembina Pipeline Corporation (PBA)

5.55%

The Williams Companies (WMB)

3.47%

Targa Resources (TRGP)

2.43%

All of these companies have yields that are considerably higher than the current 1.13% yield of the S&P 500 Index (SPY). However, these yields are quite low when compared to what some midstream master limited partnerships offer. Please consider the following companies, which are among the largest master limited partnerships in the United States:

Company Name

Current Yield

Energy Transfer (ET)

7.51%

Enterprise Products Partners (EPD)

6.92%

Plains All American Pipeline (PAA)

8.13%

Western Midstream Partners (WES)

9.10%

Hess Midstream

7.29%

MPLX (MPLX)

7.51%

Sunoco LP (SUN)

6.83%

Genesis Energy (GEL)

4.01%

As of the time of writing, the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK), which tracks the investment performance of domestic junk bonds, yields 6.55%. As we can therefore see, all of the master limited partnerships shown in the chart have higher yields than the domestic junk bond index, except for Genesis Energy. This is important because the yields shown in the chart are for common equities, not fixed-income assets. In a number of previous articles (such as this one), I have shown that equity securities will likely prove to be far superior to bonds of any type going forward due to the very high likelihood that the United States and other developed countries around the world attempt to cover the unfunded promises that their governments have made to their citizens by money printing, which will devalue the currency and cause investors who own fixed-income securities to get repaid with currencies that are worth far less than they originally lent.

Common equities are superior to bonds in this respect, and the common equities of midstream energy companies in particular could prove to be very superior to bonds. As Invesco explains in a publication from mid-2022:

Ideally, every company would like to have their revenues tied to inflation to help offset the impact of potentially increasing costs, but few do. Fortunately for the energy infrastructure sector, where nearly all projects are underwritten with long-term contracts, inflation protection is more commonplace. In March 2022, Wells Fargo Securities estimated that 60% of midstream EBITDA, or earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, is exposed to inflation escalators while our research suggests that 60% may be the lower boundary. Our conversations with companies in the sector suggest that a majority have between 60-90% of their EBITDA protected from inflation.

Thus, the contracts under which midstream companies conduct the majority of the business include provisions that result in the midstream company's revenues (and by extension, cash flow) rising alongside the price of everything else in an inflationary environment. Of course, in order for an investor to take advantage of this, they would need to be holding the common equity of the company due to the simple fact that only common equities have a link to the revenue and cash flow performance of the issuing company. Furthermore, only common equity holders receive distribution or dividend increases whenever the company's cash flows increase (and management decides to increase the payout to investors).

Thus, we see that we have the best of all worlds when it comes to midstream energy companies, as they boast both superior yields to most other assets and they should provide investors with protection against inflation. The common equity issued by master limited partnerships may be even better because of the fact that these equities have higher yields than even junk bonds. In addition to this, in the United States, the distributions paid out by master limited partnerships are taxed at a lower rate than the coupon payments of junk bonds, so investors in these securities are better off than junk bond investors all around.

It may be no surprise then that, over the past three years, midstream energy master limited partnerships have outperformed junk bonds by a sizable margin. Currently, one of the best assets available in the capital markets to track the Alerian MLP Index is the UBS ETRACS Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index ETN (MLPB). This is generally superior to the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) due to tax considerations and other reasons, which I explained in a recent article. As we can see here, this index-tracking exchange-traded note delivered an 82.22% total return over the past three years:

This was far superior to the total return provided by the junk bond index, even when we consider the fairly high yields of both assets. One reason for this is that several of the companies in the Alerian MLP Index managed to grow their cash flows and increase their distributions over the period, whereas junk bonds did not. This is linked to the fact that midstream master limited partnerships should prove to be a superior asset in an inflationary environment.

The Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation invests in a portfolio that consists of both midstream corporations and midstream master limited partnerships. The inflation protection characteristics of both types of companies are the same, as they both conduct the majority of their business under contracts with inflation adjustment clauses. The difference between them mainly revolves around the tax laws and differences in their organizational structures. As such, the fund should be able to pass through much of the inflation protection to its shareholders, and indeed this proved to be the case over the past three years. As we can see here, Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation delivered an 80.05% total return over the period, also beating domestic junk bonds:

Let us take a closer look at this fund and how it compares to its peers.

Peer Distribution Comparison

The basic business model of the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation is to purchase and hold a portfolio of midstream energy corporations and partnerships on behalf of its investors. As the fund owns the common equity of these entities, it will receive the dividends and distributions that they pay out. The fund then passes the dividends and distributions through to its shareholders. As many of these companies have fairly high yields, we might expect the fund itself to have a similarly high yield.

This is indeed the case, as shares of Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation yield 10.22% as of the time of writing. As is the case with many closed-end funds, this one has a higher yield than the indices or any of the underlying assets possess. The highest-yielding midstream company right now is Western Midstream Partners with a 9.10% yield. Most of the companies in the sector have a yield that is between 4% and 7.5% or so. There are two reasons why the fund is able to have a higher yield than the underlying assets. One of these reasons is that the fund employs leverage, much like most closed-end bond funds, which effectively boosts the yield that it receives from its portfolio overall. The second reason is that the fund pays out both the dividends and distributions that it receives (net of its expenses), along with capital gains that it earns from the portfolio. The fact that the fund pays out both capital gains and distributions is one reason why the fund's share price performance has lagged its total return by a significant extent over the past three years:

In short, with a closed-end fund like this one, the majority of the investment returns will come from the distributions as the fund pays out all of its investment profits. This means that if someone wants to actually grow the value of their investment, then they will need to reinvest some of the distributions that are received. It is a different business model than many exchange-traded funds have, as many of those funds do not pay out their capital gains but instead rely on share price appreciation.

The 10.22% yield of the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation compares pretty well with its peers, but it is not the highest-yielding energy infrastructure fund available on the market. We can see this quite clearly here:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation

Equity-MLP

10.22%

ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund (EMO)

Equity-MLP

9.15%

Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund (KYN)

Equity-MLP

7.84%

Neuberger Berman Energy Infrastructure and Income Fund (NML)

Equity-MLP

8.19%

NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund (SRV)

Equity-MLP

12.95%

As we can see, the yield of the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation is lower than that of the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund. However, it is higher than any of the other peer funds shown. This has the unfortunate effect of causing an investment in Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation to generate a lower level of income than could be achieved with the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund. For example, a $1 million investment would produce an additional $27,300 annually if it were invested in the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund compared to Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation.

However, this fund has managed to make up for the lower yield through capital appreciation over the past year. Tortoise Energy Infrastructure had the highest total return out of all of the funds shown in the chart above over the past year:

This is certainly nice, but this fund's performance is not as good if we look back over a longer period of time. For example, over the past three years, this fund was the second-worst performing one out of the peer group:

As we can see here, over the trailing three-year period, the only peer fund that delivered a worse performance was the Neuberger Berman Energy Infrastructure and Income Fund. However, we can see that Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation still managed to deliver an 80.05% total return over the period, and it is hard to complain about that, even though this fund's performance was not as good as some of the others in the peer category.

Thus, investors might be sacrificing some total return in exchange for a higher yield here, but the fund's performance over the past year did not really see investors sacrificing much of anything.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation was on March 12, 2025. There have been a few developments in the market that have had an impact on the price of this fund, which is not particularly surprising given that approximately four months have passed since we last looked at this fund.

One of the more important events that occurred came in early April, when the United States imposed a series of tariffs against many of its trading partners in an apparent attempt to reduce the perennial trade deficits that it runs with many other nations around the world. This spooked the market, as many capital market participants were uncertain about the impact that these tariffs would have on global trade or corporate profits. The markets in general dislike such uncertainty, and as such, this caused a market-wide sell-off across nearly all asset classes. Over the period from March 31, 2025, through April 4, 2025 (the first week of April), the S&P 500 Index (SP500) fell 9.58%:

Other assets were not spared either, as the spot price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil fell 13.22% and natural gas spot prices fell 6.90% over the same period. The most important assets to watch when it comes to midstream companies are generally common equities, crude oil, and natural gas, as the equity prices of these companies usually exhibit a correlation to all three assets. This is in spite of the fact that the cash flows of midstream companies are not significantly affected by the price of crude oil over the short to medium term due to the fact that they conduct business under volume-based contracts that are usually at least five years in length. Their long-term business prospects can be affected by energy prices, of course, because low prices usually prompt oil producers to invest lower amounts of money into production growth, and this means that the volumes of transported crude oil and natural gas will be lower than they would in a consistently high price environment that is conducive to upstream production growth. Another factor influencing the price of midstream equities is that low crude oil prices generally reduce the interest that investors have in purchasing the equities of any company in the oil sector, and lower demand for these equities tends to weigh on the price. In other words, the fact that both common equities and energy commodities sold off in April 2025 is a sharp negative for midstream common equities.

The S&P 500 Index has recovered from the early April sell-off, due mostly to investors realizing that the tariffs that actually end up getting imposed will be less than they feared. In addition, multiple investment banks and other Wall Street firms have stepped back from their recession predictions, with J.P. Morgan now predicting that there is only a 40% chance that the United States will be in a recession by the end of 2025. The bank was projecting that there was a 60% probability of a 2025 recession back in April. However, crude oil and natural gas have not recovered from the losses that they suffered during the broad-market sell-off. As of right now, the spot price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil is down 8.72% and the spot price of natural gas at Henry Hub is down 25.28% compared to the prices that these two commodities had on March 31, 2025:

The prices of both hydrocarbon commodities are also down from the levels that they had on the date of our March 12, 2025, discussion. We can see that here:

As shown, the spot price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil is down 3.65% and the spot price of natural gas at Henry Hub is down 24.75% since that date. We can expect that this weakness in energy prices will weigh somewhat on the performance of midstream common equities despite the recovery in the broader market.

As such, we can make the assumption that the recent performance of the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Fund has been weaker than that of the S&P 500 Index since our last discussion on the fund. This is indeed the case, as shares of Tortoise Energy Infrastructure are only up 2.62% since that date:

This compares quite poorly with the 13.57% gain of the S&P 500 Index over the same period. Admittedly, this is rather disappointing, but it is also as good as can really be expected given the weak performance of crude oil over the period in question.

As might be expected, the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation did somewhat better on a total return basis. We can see that here:

As we can see, Tortoise Energy Infrastructure has delivered a 7.24% total return since the last time that we discussed this fund. This is still worse than the 14.10% total return of the S&P 500 Index (SP500TR), but it is certainly better than the 2.62% return that was suggested by a look at the price performance alone.

Overall, the performance of the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation over the past four months does little to make a case for it. However, it is the best that we can hope for given that oil prices today are too low to encourage upstream producers in the United States' various shale plays to grow their production, and thus limiting the growth prospects of many midstream companies.

About The Fund

The website for the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of total return. This objective makes a great deal of sense given that the fund is primarily an equity fund. The fund's most recent holdings report, which is dated February 28, 2025, provides the following asset allocation for this fund:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Common Stocks

99.1%

Master Limited Partnerships

27.1%

Private Investments

0.0%

Money Market Funds

0.0%

As we can see, nearly all of the fund's assets were invested in common equities or master limited partnerships on February 28, 2025. The fund did have a single investment in a private company, TK NYS Solar Holdco LLC, as of that date, but these are listed with a value of $0 as of that date:

The fund originally invested $52,593,181 in order to acquire this position, which would represent 6.32% of the fund's net assets if it were still worth that much. The fund's notes to the holdings report state the following:

(b) Fair value determined significant unobservable inputs in accordance with procedures established by and under the supervision of the Adviser, acting as Valuation Designee. These securities represented $0 or 0.0% of net assets as of February 28, 2025.(c) Restricted securities have a total fair value of $0 which represents less than 0.0% of net assets.(d) Deemed to be an affiliate of the fund.

TK NYS Solar Holdco was attempting to generate electricity in the state of Kansas, according to its Bloomberg profile. Many solar companies, particularly smaller ones that lack the support of a larger utility, have struggled financially in recent years, as the industry has had a very hard time turning a profit in the face of rising interest rates. That could be the case here, as the fund has clearly written off the value of this position down to zero, which suggests that its management believes that the company will not ever be able to generate a profit or manage to sell whatever assets it may possess. Thus, the fund essentially lost $52,593,181 on a renewable energy play gone bad, which makes it yet another casualty of the troubles that the renewable energy industry has had for quite some time now.

The fund's master limited partnership positions are mostly ones that are mentioned in the introduction:

We see here that the fund's holdings include Western Midstream Partners, Energy Transfer, Enterprise Products Partners, and MPLX, all of which have yields that are substantially above the yields of most common equities. We also see XPLR Infrastructure (XIFR), which is another company that has been negatively impacted by the challenges facing the renewable energy sector. The company suspended its distribution earlier this year, which was disappointing given that it had previously increased it every quarter, with the company's distribution history showing that it increased it for every quarter from late 2014 through late 2024. The company's common equity is down 65.10% over the past twelve months:

XPLR Infrastructure does have a relationship with NextEra Energy (NEE) and acts as something of a drop-down master limited partnership for that company, so this company may eventually recover from its recent weakness, but there is no guarantee that this will be the case. For now, though, the cancellation of the distribution has reduced the fund's income, and it is almost certainly sitting on some unrealized losses from the equity decline. After all, the fund's semi-annual report dated May 31, 2024, does show that the fund had a position in XPLR Infrastructure (then called NextEra Energy Partners), so it appears that this fund held its position in this company over the past year and thus suffered the capital destruction that the company delivered over the trailing twelve-month period.

Valuation

Shares of the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation are currently trading at an 8.12% discount on net asset value. This is a better price than the 5.43% discount on net asset value that the fund has had on average over the past month.

While the fund's current valuation is fairly attractive when compared to its one-month average price, it is a lot more expensive than the fund's average price over the past three years. Over the trailing three-year period, the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation has traded at prices ranging from a 4.08% discount to a 20.81% discount on net asset value. The average price over the trailing three-year period has been a 15.12% discount, which is a much better price than what the fund has right now.

Expenses

The most recent annual report for the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation states that the fund had an expense ratio of 2.90% for the twelve-month period that ended on November 30, 2024. As is usually the case with closed-end funds, a significant proportion of this fund's expenses comes from the fact that it has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage for its investment portfolio. If we exclude these expenses, the fund only had an expense ratio of 1.69%.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation is one way to add midstream companies to a portfolio and earn a higher yield than any company in the sector possesses. Midstream companies do a pretty good job of protecting investors against inflation, and given that inflation might be a problem going forward, this is a rather appealing characteristic. The fund has, unfortunately, taken some losses recently due to the struggles that have been plaguing the renewable energy sector, but at least the traditional midstream sector has been performing strongly enough to offset these losses and still deliver investors an attractive total return.
